skill.name: genkit
skill.description: Build Firebase Genkit AI workflows with type-safe flows, tool-calling agents, RAG pipelines, streaming responses, prompt files, and Firebase or Cloud Run deployment patterns.
skill.summary: Build Firebase Genkit flows, agents, RAG pipelines, and deployable AI services.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, network, and environment variable patterns. Review found most hits are markdown fences, TypeScript examples, localhost or documentation links, and placeholder API key setup guidance. The real concern is copyable install and deployment commands, especially the curl-to-bash installer pattern, so publication should include a warning.

medium_findings.title: Copyable Pipe-to-Shell Installer Command
medium_findings.description: The skill includes curl -sL cli.genkit.dev | bash as an installation option. This is a real risky pattern because it executes remote content directly in the user shell, even though it appears to document a legitimate Genkit installer.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is present exactly as a remote script piped into bash. The surrounding setup context is legitimate documentation, so confidence is high for risk but not for malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Copyable Deployment Commands Handle Secrets
medium_findings.description: The skill shows API key exports, Firebase secret configuration, and a Cloud Run command that passes an API key from the shell environment. These are common setup patterns, but users could expose secrets through shell history, logs, or misconfigured deployment settings.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations clearly discuss API keys and environment variables. They are examples and include advice not to hardcode keys, so this is an operational handling risk rather than credential theft.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks and Template Literals
low_findings.description: The static analyzer interpreted many markdown code fences, inline code spans, and TypeScript template literals as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are documentation examples in SKILL.md and SKILL.toon, not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows the reported backticks occur inside markdown examples or TypeScript strings. There is no standalone script file or runtime hook that would execute them as skill code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation and Localhost URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are example media, localhost Developer UI references, official Genkit documentation links, and the project repository. No evidence found that the skill sends data to these URLs during skill activation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in explanatory text, references, and sample inputs. No fetch, upload, webhook, or hidden network exfiltration behavior was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto and C2 Keywords
low_findings.description: Static blocker hits for weak cryptography and C2 keywords appear to match ordinary Genkit documentation terms, model names, examples, and document labels. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation, command and control behavior, or malware-style instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are normal AI workflow descriptions or examples. They do not define encryption routines, covert channels, persistence, beacons, or remote operator instructions.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Dangerous Combination Heuristic
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined command examples, URLs, and API key setup text into a suspicious pattern. Manual review found these elements are documentation examples, not an integrated code path for credential access, network transfer, or command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The same files contain command, URL, and API key examples, which explains the heuristic. No hidden automation, obfuscation, or exfiltration flow was found during semantic review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Script Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The installation section includes a curl command piped directly to bash. Users should prefer package manager installation or verify the script before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell syntax is directly present and copyable. The context is installer documentation, not hidden execution by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Tool Execution in Agent Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The agent example enables automatic tool execution. This is a normal Genkit feature, but production tools should validate inputs and constrain external side effects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly enables tool auto-execution. The example tools are benign stubs, so this is a design caution rather than confirmed abuse.

content.value_statement: Teams need reliable patterns for AI flows, agents, RAG, streaming, and deployment. This skill provides Genkit examples and guidance for building type-safe AI services.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Firebase Genkit for flows, agents, RAG, streaming, and deployment.
Shows TypeScript examples for defining flows with Zod input and output schemas.
Demonstrates model provider setup for Gemini, Vertex AI, OpenAI, Anthropic, and Ollama.
Provides patterns for Dotprompt files, tool calling, chat sessions, and multi-agent orchestration.
Documents Genkit Developer UI commands, flow execution, evaluation, and deployment options.
limitations: The skill is documentation-focused and does not install Genkit or create project files by itself.
Most examples are TypeScript-first, with only high-level mention of Go and Python support.
Commands may need updates for local package versions, cloud projects, and authentication settings.
Users must review remote installer commands and secret handling before running copied commands.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Genkit Flow
use_cases.description: Create a typed flow with validation, model calls, and clear output handling for a new AI feature.

use_cases.title: Design a RAG Service
use_cases.description: Plan document indexing, retrieval, grounded generation, and response schemas for a knowledge base assistant.

use_cases.title: Prepare Cloud Deployment
use_cases.description: Map a Genkit flow to Firebase Functions, Express, or Cloud Run with environment and secret configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single AI endpoint.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Genkit skill to design a basic TypeScript flow for this task: [task]. Include schemas, model call, and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Add Tool Calling
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate agent workflow with controlled tools.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Genkit skill to add tool calling to my flow. My tools are [tools]. Show schemas, validation, and safe execution boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a RAG Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced retrieval and grounded generation design.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Genkit skill to design a RAG pipeline for [documents]. Include indexing, retrieval, prompt grounding, and evaluation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy and Operate
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Genkit skill to prepare my flow for [Firebase Functions or Cloud Run]. Review secrets, streaming, tracing, and deployment commands.

output_examples.input: Design a summarization flow for support tickets.
output: A flow design with ticket text as input and summary, priority, and next action as output.
Zod schema guidance for validation and clear errors when generation returns no output.
Developer UI and flow run steps for local testing.

output_examples.input: Add search tools to an agent that answers product questions.
output: Tool definitions with typed inputs, typed outputs, and bounded search behavior.
Agent flow guidance that separates retrieval from response generation.
Safety notes for validating user queries before tool execution.

output_examples.input: Prepare a Genkit RAG flow for Cloud Run.
output: Deployment checklist covering environment variables, secrets, and region settings.
Recommended tracing, evaluation, and streaming checks before release.
Warnings about verifying installer scripts and protecting API keys.

best_practices: Define Zod input and output schemas for every flow before adding model calls.
Use environment secrets and deployment-specific secret managers for provider keys.
Test flows in the Genkit Developer UI with realistic inputs before deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not run remote installer scripts without reviewing or verifying the downloaded content.
Do not hardcode API keys or paste secrets into source files, prompts, or shared logs.
Do not auto-execute tools that can change data or call external systems without validation.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps design Firebase Genkit flows, agents, RAG pipelines, streaming responses, prompt files, and deployment patterns.

faq.question: Does it generate complete applications?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and examples. You still need to adapt code to your project, dependencies, and cloud environment.

faq.question: Which model providers are covered?
faq.answer: The skill references Gemini, Vertex AI, OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, AWS Bedrock, DeepSeek, and xAI plugins.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a warning. The main concern is copyable shell and secret-handling commands in documentation examples.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What should I review before running commands?
faq.answer: Review remote installer scripts, package names, project identifiers, region settings, and all API key handling.
